era_context: Late 14th century: Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China; Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans; Western Europe experiences the Great Schism (1378–1417); the Timurid Renaissance develops in Central Asia; the Joseon Dynasty rules Korea; the Aztec Empire continues expansion in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major chronicle centers

events.title: Battle of the Piani dei Santi (Papal-Neapolitan Conflict)
events.region: Italy (Papal States and Kingdom of Naples)
events.description: The Battle of the Piani dei Santi was fought between papal forces and the Kingdom of Naples during the War of the Eight Saints (1375–1378) and its aftermath. The battle was part of the broader conflict between the Papacy and various Italian powers over territorial control and political authority. The conflict reflected the instability of the Italian peninsula during the Great Schism period. The specific outcome of this battle is not always clearly documented in surviving sources.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Piani dei Santi is documented in Italian chronicles, but the exact date and details are uncertain. The year 1380 is assigned based on the broader chronology of papal-Neapolitan conflicts.

events.title: Ottoman Expansion in the Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I continued their systematic expansion in the Balkans, consolidating control over Bulgarian and Serbian territories. In 1380, the Ottomans were engaged in campaigns to subjugate remaining independent Balkan principalities and to establish Ottoman administrative control. These campaigns were part of the broader Ottoman expansion that would eventually lead to the conquest of Constantinople in 1453.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans in 1380 is documented in later Ottoman chronicles. The specific campaigns of 1380 are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.

events.title: Timur's Consolidation of Power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) continued to consolidate his power in Central Asia, establishing Samarkand as his capital and center of the Timurid Renaissance. In 1380, he was engaged in campaigns to secure his control over the Silk Road trade routes and to establish his authority over rival Central Asian powers. These efforts laid the foundation for his later expansion into the Middle East and beyond.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (biography of Timur, written early 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power in Central Asia in 1380 is documented in later biographical sources. The specific events of 1380 are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Consolidation
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Hongwu Emperor continued to consolidate central authority and eliminate remaining Yuan Dynasty remnants. In 1380, the Ming government was engaged in reorganizing provincial administration, establishing the examination system for civil service, and strengthening the imperial bureaucracy. These reforms were crucial to the stability and longevity of the Ming Dynasty. The year 1380 marks a period of significant administrative reorganization.
events.certainty_note: Ming administrative consolidation in 1380 is documented in official records. The specific reforms of this year are part of a broader process of consolidation that occurred throughout the 1370s and 1380s.

events.title: Wycliffe's Reformist Activity
events.description: John Wycliffe, the English theologian and reformer, continued his advocacy for church reform and the translation of the Bible into English. In 1380, he was in his final years of life (he died in 1384). His ideas were spreading through his followers, the Lollards, who would continue his reformist agenda after his death. Wycliffe's work challenged papal authority and advocated for a more accessible, vernacular Christianity.

sources.name: Knighton's Chronicle (Henry Knighton, contemporary English chronicler)

events.certainty_note: Wycliffe's continued reformist activity in 1380 is documented in his own writings and in contemporary chronicles. The date is confirmed.

events.title: Joseon Dynasty Consolidation in Korea
events.description: The Joseon Dynasty, which had been established in 1392, was in its early period of consolidation. However, 1380 predates the Joseon Dynasty's founding. This entry reflects the late Goryeo Dynasty period, which was characterized by political instability and the rise of the military general Yi Seong-gye, who would found the Joseon Dynasty in 1392. In 1380, Korea was transitioning from Goryeo to Joseon rule.
key_figures: Yi Seong-gye (future founder of Joseon Dynasty, b. 1335)

sources.name: Samguk Sagi and Samguk Yusa (Korean historical records)
Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty, compiled later)

events.certainty_note: The transition from Goryeo to Joseon occurred in 1392, so 1380 falls within the late Goryeo period. The specific events of 1380 are not always clearly documented in surviving Korean sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Piani dei Santi is not well-documented in surviving sources, and its exact date and significance are uncertain. Some historians question whether this battle occurred in 1380 or in a different year. The entry on Joseon Dynasty consolidation is misleading, as the Joseon Dynasty was not founded until 1392; 1380 falls within the late Goryeo period. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of the other events listed, but dating precision is limited.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1380 is moderate but regionally uneven. European sources (English, Italian, Ottoman) are relatively abundant, though Ottoman records are often later compilations. Chinese sources are rich. Korean sources are fragmentary for this period. Middle Eastern sources are limited. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania are essentially undocumented. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. European and Chinese sources may reflect court biases and the perspectives of the literate elite.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans in 1380 continued in 1381.
Timur's consolidation in Central Asia in 1380 continued in 1381.
The late Goryeo period in 1380 led to the founding of the Joseon Dynasty in 1392.
